item.title: “And how, monks, is dwelling heedfully? If one lives with restraint of the eye faculty, monks, his mind is not defiled by forms cognizable by…
item.description:
“And how, monks, is dwelling heedfully? If one lives with restraint of the eye faculty, monks, his mind is not defiled by forms cognizable by the eye. For one with an undefiled mind, gladness arises. With gladness, rapture arises. With rapture, the body becomes tranquil. With a tranquil body, he experiences ease. For one at ease, the mind becomes concentrated. When the mind is concentrated, phenomena are clearly seen. When phenomena are clearly seen, he is called ‘one who dwells heedfully’ … the ear faculty … the nose faculty … the tongue faculty … the body faculty … If one lives with restraint of the mind faculty, his mind is not defiled by phenomena cognizable by the mind. For one with an undefiled mind, gladness arises … he is called ‘one who dwells heedfully.’ Thus, monks, is dwelling heedfully.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.97
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Migajāla, there are phenomena cognizable by the mind that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk delights in them, praises…
item.description:
“Migajāla, there are phenomena cognizable by the mind that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk delights in them, praises them, and dwells with lust for them, then because he delights, praises, and dwells with lust, delight arises. With the arising of delight, lust arises. With the arising of lust, bondage arises. Bound by the bondage of delight, Migajāla, that monk is called ‘one who dwells with a companion.’ Even if he lives in remote lodgings, in forests, in groves, in quiet places, with little sound, little noise, free from people’s breath, secluded, suitable for seclusion — still he is called ‘one who dwells with a companion.’ Why? Because craving, the companion, has not been abandoned by him. Therefore he is called ‘one who dwells with a companion.’”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.63
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Householder, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk does not delight in…
item.description:
“Householder, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk does not delight in those forms, does not praise them, and dwells without lust for them, not depending on them, consciousness arises without clinging to them. Householder, a monk without clinging attains complete Nibbāna … Householder, there are tastes … Householder, there are phenomena cognizable by the mind that are desirable … enticing. If a monk does not delight in those phenomena, does not praise them, and dwells without lust for them — because he does not delight, does not praise, and dwells without lust, not depending on them, consciousness arises without clinging to them. Without clinging, householder, that monk attains Nibbāna. This, householder, is the cause, this is the reason why some beings here and now attain complete Nibbāna.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.124
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And how, monks, is a monk one who sees sensual pleasures? When sensual pleasures are seen, then desire, lust for sensual pleasure, infatuation with sensual…
item.description:
“And how, monks, is a monk one who sees sensual pleasures? When sensual pleasures are seen, then desire, lust for sensual pleasure, infatuation with sensual pleasure, and fever of sensual pleasure have no underlying tendency regarding sensual pleasures. Suppose, monks, there is a pit of glowing embers deeper than a man’s height, filled with glowing embers that are neither flaming nor smoking. Then a man comes who desires to live, not to die, who desires happiness and loathes suffering. Two strong men grab him by both arms and drag him toward that pit. He twists his body this way and that. Why? Because he knows: ‘I will fall into this pit of embers. Because of this, I will meet death or deadly suffering.’ So too, monks, a monk sees sensual pleasures as a pit of embers. And one who sees sensual pleasures thus has no desire, lust, infatuation, or fever for sensual pleasures as an underlying tendency.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.244
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And how, good sirs, is one not defiled by sensual lust? Here, good sirs, when a monk sees a form with the eye, he is…
item.description:
“And how, good sirs, is one not defiled by sensual lust? Here, good sirs, when a monk sees a form with the eye, he is not attached to agreeable forms nor repelled by disagreeable forms. He dwells with bodily mindfulness established, with an immeasurable mind. He knows as it actually is the liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom. Here, evil, unwholesome states that have arisen are abandoned without remainder. When he tastes a flavor with the tongue … When he cognizes a phenomenon with the mind, he is not attached to agreeable phenomena nor repelled by disagreeable phenomena. He dwells with bodily mindfulness established, with an immeasurable mind. He knows as it actually is the liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom. Here, evil, unwholesome states that have arisen are abandoned without remainder. This, good sirs, is called a monk who is not defiled by sensual lust regarding forms … regarding tastes … regarding phenomena. A monk living thus, good sirs, if Māra approaches him through the eye, Māra does not gain an opportunity, Māra does not gain an object. If Māra approaches him through the tongue … through the mind, Māra does not gain an opportunity, Māra does not gain an object.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.243
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, on King Snowy Mountain there are rough, uneven stretches where neither monkeys nor humans go. On King Snowy Mountain there are rough, uneven stretches…
item.description:
“Monks, on King Snowy Mountain there are rough, uneven stretches where neither monkeys nor humans go. On King Snowy Mountain there are rough, uneven stretches where monkeys go but humans do not. On King Snowy Mountain there are even, pleasant stretches where both monkeys and humans go. There, monks, hunters set a trap of sticky resin on the paths where monkeys go, to catch monkeys. Here, monks, those monkeys that are not foolish and not greedy avoid the trap of resin when they see it. But a foolish, greedy monkey, seeing the resin, reaches out with its hand and gets stuck. Wanting to free its hand, it reaches out with the other hand and gets stuck. Wanting to free both hands, it reaches out with one foot and gets stuck. Wanting to free its hands and foot, it reaches out with the other foot and gets stuck. Wanting to free its hands and feet, it puts its mouth to it and gets stuck. Thus, monks, the monkey, caught in five places, lies down screeching, having fallen into misfortune, into calamity, subject to the hunter’s will. The hunter then kills it, roasts it over a charcoal fire, and goes away as he wishes. So too, monks, for one who wanders outside his own proper range, into the domain of others. For one who wanders outside his proper range, into the domain of others, Māra gains an opportunity, Māra gains an object.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 47.7
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Furthermore, monks, a noble disciple reflects: ‘Whatever sensual pleasures present and future, whatever perceptions of sensual pleasure present and future, whatever forms present and future…
item.description:
“Furthermore, monks, a noble disciple reflects: ‘Whatever sensual pleasures present and future, whatever perceptions of sensual pleasure present and future, whatever forms present and future — all are the four great elements and form derived from the four great elements.’ While he practices thus, dwells often thus, his mind becomes serene in that element. With serene mind, he attains the Imperturbable (āneñja) either in this very life or he becomes strongly inclined to wisdom. At the breakup of the body, after death, it is possible that his consciousness, proceeding accordingly, may attain the Imperturbable.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 106 – Āneñjasappāyasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Nisabha: Having abandoned the five strands of sensual pleasure, Those things so agreeable, Which delight the mind — With faith, I went forth, Putting…
item.description:
Elder Nisabha:

Having abandoned the five strands of sensual pleasure,
Those things so agreeable,
Which delight the mind —
With faith, I went forth,
Putting an end to suffering.

I do not delight in death,
I do not delight in life.
I await the time,
Mindful and clearly comprehending.

From: Theragāthā 2.38
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “What leads the world along? What torments itself? And what is the one thing Upon which everything depends?” “Craving leads the world along, Craving torments…
item.description:
“What leads the world along?
What torments itself?
And what is the one thing
Upon which everything depends?”

“Craving leads the world along,
Craving torments itself,
Craving is the one thing
Upon which everything depends.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 1.63
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Kappa: Full of various impurities, Where there is no purity — Like a long-stagnant puddle, A great boil, a wound. Full of blood and…
item.description:
Elder Kappa:

Full of various impurities,
Where there is no purity —
Like a long-stagnant puddle,
A great boil, a wound.

Full of blood and pus,
Sunk in a pit of filth —
This body oozes fluid,
Constantly oozing impurity.

Bound by sixty sinews,
Plastered with flesh as mortar,
Clad in skin as armor —
This foul body is useless.

Connected by a chain of bones,
Tied together with sinews —
By their combined power,
They create various postures.

Always heading toward death,
Approaching the end of life —
There, abandoned,
People go according to their desire.

The body, shrouded by ignorance,
Bound by four bonds,
Sinks in the flood,
Caught in the net of underlying tendencies —

Fettered by five hindrances,
Obsessed by thought,
Following the root of craving,
Covered by the veil of delusion.

Thus this body wanders,
Led by the chariot of kamma —
Sometimes success, sometimes failure —
Enduring many births of craving.

One who thinks ‘this body is mine’
Is a foolish worldling,
Increasing the fearful cemetery,
Clinging tightly to renewed existence.

One who avoids this body
As one avoids a snake smeared with feces —
That one abandons the root of existence
And attains Nibbāna, free from defilements.

From: Theragāthā 10.5
📖 Read this sutta
